sector: Industrials — Specialty Electric/Infrastructure EPC & Engineering Services
bull_thesis: Quanta is the indispensable labor and execution layer beneath the single largest physical bottleneck in the artificial-intelligence build-out: the electric grid. Every dollar that hyperscalers and governments spend on AI computing capacity ultimately needs power delivered through high-voltage transmission lines, substations, and distribution networks — and Quanta is the largest, most capable specialty-trades labor platform in North America to build that infrastructure. The evidence is broad and self-reinforcing. First-quarter 2026 revenue came in at $7.87 billion, up 26% year over year, with adjusted earnings per share of $2.68, up 51% — roughly twice the operating leverage of the revenue growth. The company's backlog hit a record $48.5 billion at roughly 1.6 times the value of work it books versus what it completes — multi-year visibility, not a one-quarter pop. Critically, fewer than 25% of the new backlog comes from the marquee partnership with American Electric Power on 765-kilovolt transmission, which means demand is broad-based across many utilities, not dependent on a single contract. The competitive moat is skilled-labor scarcity: as management puts it, a journeyman electrician can train another journeyman, but money alone cannot replicate that. Quanta adds 5,000 to 6,000 journeymen organically each year and is the only firm that can credibly promise utilities certainty on execution, labor, and supply chain — a bundled guarantee that supports premium pricing. The company is now integrating vertically into long-lead equipment such as transformers (currently 36-month lead times) and high-voltage gear, widening that moat further. The data-center opportunity — described by management as a market growing more than 100% annually and already a large business after only about 18 months — sits on top of a defensible electrification core that would grow regardless of artificial intelligence. Full-year guidance has been raised, and the beat-and-raise track record is strong. Quanta is on the winning side of every major secular force in its market, with no visible terminal threat. The case rests on continued utility and AI capital-spending durability, ongoing skilled-labor recruitment, and improving free-cash-flow conversion off the initial 2026 guidance.
bear_thesis: Quanta is a thin-margin, moderate-return engineering, procurement, and construction (EPC) labor business wearing a hyper-growth stock multiple. Operating margin runs around 5.6%, net margin around 3.7%, and return on invested capital (the profit the business earns relative to the capital tied up in it) sits at roughly 8.5% — barely above the estimated cost of that capital. Yet the stock trades at approximately 49 times forward earnings and 42 times forward earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization (EBITDA). The AI-grid opportunity is not a hidden discovery; it is fully and aggressively priced in, which means any stumble creates asymmetric downside. The cash economics are weaker than headline earnings suggest. The initial 2026 free-cash-flow guide is just $500 million against roughly $2 billion in net income — growth is consuming working capital — so reported earnings overstate the cash the business actually distributes. The free-cash-flow yield at the current price is below 0.5%. Management's stated primary use of capital is acquisitions, introducing integration risk and potential goodwill write-downs if the cycle cools. The core demand driver — hyperscaler and utility AI capital spending — is the same single factor underpinning the entire scale-out investment theme, so Quanta offers no independent source of return relative to a basket already saturated with that exposure. The marquee catalyst, the American Electric Power 765-kilovolt partnership, already fired in November 2025, and the stock has already run to an all-time high of $785 in May 2026. Gas-generation backlog is gated on air permits described as arriving in the later half of the year and beyond — a recurring deferral. A single weak free-cash-flow print, a backlog deceleration toward 1.5 times or below, or any pause in AI capital spending could compress a 49-times multiple sharply. The warning signs to watch: 2026 free cash flow landing at the low end of guidance, the book-to-bill ratio (the value of new contracts won versus work completed) slipping toward 1.0, or an earnings miss.
sop_bull: $820 to $870 per share. Because Quanta does not cleanly separate its segments — the Renewables business folded into Electric Infrastructure Solutions — a clean sum-of-the-parts (valuing each business line separately) is not available, so this is a whole-company intrinsic estimate. Applying a generous but defensible multiple of approximately 30 times forward 2027 EBITDA of an estimated $4.3 to $4.5 billion (compounding roughly 20% off the 2026 guidance midpoint of $3.49 to $3.65 billion) produces an enterprise value of about $130 to $135 billion. Subtracting roughly $6 billion in net debt (total borrowings minus cash) and dividing by approximately 150 million shares yields around $830 to $860. An earnings cross-check at 58 to 60 times the 2026 adjusted earnings-per-share guide of $14.25, ramping to roughly $17 in 2027, also points to $850 or above. The bull case leans on MasTec-cohort premium multiples and credits Quanta's scale and quality advantage. The entire bull value rests on multiple persistence and 20%-plus EBITDA compounding continuing without interruption.
sop_bear: $360 to $430 per share. A whole-company intrinsic estimate at a quality-appropriate multiple. This is an approximately 5.6% operating-margin, 8.5% return-on-invested-capital contractor; peers such as Primoris (PRIM) trade at roughly 16 to 18 times EBITDA. Applying 18 to 20 times the 2026 adjusted EBITDA of approximately $3.55 billion produces an enterprise value of $64 to $71 billion. After subtracting $6 billion in net debt and dividing by 150 million shares, the result is roughly $385 to $435. An earnings cross-check using 28 to 30 times (the MasTec-to-Primoris range) on $14.25 adjusted earnings per share reaches $400 to $430. Adjusting further for the free-cash-flow gap — applying a 2.5% free-cash-flow yield appropriate for a cyclical EPC contractor against $500 million of 2026 free cash flow — produces an extreme, punitive case around $135. The bear central estimate is roughly $400. No special-situation overlay applies: net debt is real, and acquisition-led capital allocation is a use of cash, not a return of it.
sop_breakdown: Whole-company intrinsic — segments are not separately valuable, and the Electric Infrastructure Solutions division, at roughly 81% of revenue with a 10.3% operating margin, is the quality core alongside Underground and Infrastructure at roughly 19% and a 7.3% margin. The base case applies roughly 22 to 24 times EBITDA to the 2026 adjusted EBITDA midpoint of approximately $3.55 billion, producing an enterprise value of $78 to $85 billion. After subtracting roughly $6 billion in net debt and dividing by approximately 150 million shares, the result is $480 to $525 per share. An earnings cross-check at 34 to 37 times the 2026 adjusted earnings-per-share guide of $14.25 reaches $485 to $525 — a premium to Primoris at roughly 28 times and roughly in line with MasTec at roughly 40 times, discounted for Quanta's thinner free-cash-flow conversion. The multiple is set above the pure-EPC peer group to credit Quanta's scale and labor moat and backlog durability, but well below the current roughly 49 times because return on invested capital at roughly 8.5% and free cash flow of $500 million in 2026 do not support franchise-tier pricing. No net-cash or asset-sale overlay applies given roughly $6 billion in net debt.
risk_reward: Poor, and negative at the current price of $702. Downside to the base fair-value estimate of roughly $505 is approximately 28%; downside to the bear case of roughly $400 is approximately 43%. Upside to fair value is negative — the stock already trades above the base estimate. A gain exists only in the bull scenario of roughly $830, which requires flawless execution and the current high multiple holding, offering about 18% upside. At $702 the asymmetry is roughly one unit of upside against one-and-a-half to two-and-a-half units of downside. Risk/reward is unfavorable — patience is rewarded; chasing is not.
peer_comps_note: The true peer group is the grid and EPC and electrical-equipment cohort. Live multiples as of June 2026, verified from public sources: MasTec (MTZ) at roughly 40 times forward earnings and 26 times EBITDA on a roughly $28 billion market cap — the closest pure peer, with similar AI-grid and transmission exposure, comparable thin EPC margins, and a raised 2026 earnings guide of plus 33%. Primoris (PRIM) at roughly 28 times forward earnings and 16 to 18 times EBITDA — smaller, and it cut its 2026 adjusted earnings guide by 17%, a reminder that the EPC cohort is not uniformly strong. GE Vernova (GEV) at roughly 56 times earnings — adjacent rather than direct (high-voltage equipment, turbines, and grid hardware higher up the value chain, with structurally better margins and returns). Eaton (ETN) and Emerson (EMR at roughly 19 times forward earnings) are electrical-equipment manufacturers — higher quality, higher margin, lower multiple than Quanta, and a useful sanity anchor showing how much of a premium Quanta carries. Within the grid layer: Quanta ranks first on scale, backlog durability at $48.5 billion with a 1.6 times book-to-bill, and breadth of AI-grid exposure. It ranks in the middle on growth (comparable to MasTec). It ranks in the bottom quarter on margins (roughly 5.6% operating versus Eaton and Emerson in the mid-teens to low twenties) and returns on invested capital at roughly 8.5%. And it carries the richest valuation of the EPC pure-plays at roughly 49 times forward earnings and 42 times EBITDA versus MasTec at 40 times and 26 times and Primoris at 28 times and 17 times. The premium over peers is justified by moat and scale, but the AI-grid optionality is the most fully paid for of the grid EPC group.
role_in_scaleout: Quanta is the primary skilled-trades labor, execution, and increasingly long-lead-equipment EPC platform that physically builds the high-voltage transmission lines, substations, and grid interconnects that every AI data center requires to receive power. It is the largest such labor pool in North America, making it a near-mandatory counterparty in the grid build-out. Demand is highly durable — the grid must roughly double in size regardless of artificial intelligence, which merely accelerates the timeline — but the value the business captures lies in scarce skilled labor rather than high returns on invested capital.
consensus_delta: Wall Street is not looking the wrong way here — it is looking exactly the right way, and the market has already fully priced that view in. The consensus narrative of a record backlog, an AI-grid super-cycle, and a beat-and-raise track record is correct, which is precisely the problem: at roughly 49 times forward earnings and 42 times EBITDA there is no disagreement with the market left to exploit. Where we disagree with the market runs in the opposite direction. Two facts are under-weighted by the street. First, the 2026 free-cash-flow guide of only roughly $500 million against roughly $2 billion in net income means headline earnings materially overstate the cash the business actually generates. The free-cash-flow yield at this price is below 0.5%, and a 49-times multiple on a sub-1% free-cash-flow contractor is essentially a bet that 20%-plus compounding continues uninterrupted for years. Second, return on invested capital at roughly 8.5% sits barely above the cost of that capital. This is a scale and labor moat that protects revenue and competitive position — but it is not a franchise earning excess returns, yet the stock is priced as though it were. The bull assumption embedded in street estimates — that the book-to-bill ratio stays at 1.5 to 1.6 times and EBITDA grows roughly 20% annually with margin expansion for three to five years — is plausible, but it is the base case, not an upside surprise. There is no hidden positive thesis the market is missing. The asymmetry, if anything, is to the downside if book-to-bill, free-cash-flow conversion, or AI capital-spending pace disappoints.
valley_of_death: Near-term risks over the next three to nine months are valuation and cash risks, not solvency risks — the balance sheet is healthy at roughly $6 billion in net debt and roughly 1.45 times debt-to-EBITDA. The specific pressure points are: first, free-cash-flow conversion — the 2026 guide of roughly $500 million is well below 2025's $1.67 billion, and if a quarterly print confirms continued working-capital drag without an inflection, a 49-times multiple on deteriorating cash conversion becomes acutely vulnerable. Second, a deceleration in the book-to-bill ratio — the entire thesis depends on backlog compounding; any print below roughly 1.3 times would be read as the super-cycle peaking and could de-rate the multiple sharply. Third, gas-generation permitting slipping again — combined-cycle backlog is gated on air permits described as arriving in the later half of 2026 and beyond; continued deferral disappoints. Fourth, an AI capital-spending pause — the regime analysis flags AI capital spending as driving roughly 40% of 2026 earnings growth across the S&P 500 and as the single dominant shared factor in this investment theme; any hyperscaler guidance cut would hit Quanta directly and trigger broad de-risking of the entire scale-out group, with Quanta's 49-times multiple having the most room to fall. The stock already printed an all-time high of $785 in May 2026 and sits roughly 10% below it — a richly valued, momentum-owned name into a complacent, fully-invested market environment is exactly the profile that gaps down sharply on a single soft data point.
positioning_washout: Quanta is a momentum and growth-quality name owned by growth funds, AI-thematic funds, and passive index flows — it is a member of the S&P 500 at roughly $105 billion in market cap — not by yield or value investors. The dividend yield is roughly 0.06%, so there is no yield-oriented holder base to flush mechanically. The de-rating risk is the opposite: this is a crowded long in a stretched-positioning environment where, on any thesis wobble around free cash flow or book-to-bill, growth and thematic holders and trend-following funds would de-risk together. With global fund manager cash at a 3.9% level (a contrarian sell signal per Bank of America's monthly survey) and the index roughly 30% concentrated in the seven largest technology stocks, there is little fresh capital sitting on the sidelines to absorb a sell-off. The forced-selling pressure described here is therefore a future risk to be waited for, not a present opportunity that has already cleared — positions are at the rich end of the range, not washed out.
forcing_function: No positive catalyst that forces a re-rating remains untapped. The marquee event — the American Electric Power 765-kilovolt strategic partnership — already fired in November 2025 and is already in the backlog. The upcoming dated events are tests rather than unlocks: the second-quarter 2026 earnings report, expected in late July 2026, which will show the next book-to-bill and free-cash-flow-conversion prints; gas-generation permit approvals in the later half of 2026; and any incremental hyperscaler or utility mega-award, which would extend but not re-rate the story given established precedent. The realistic catalyst that forces a re-rating is to the downside: the first quarter that book-to-bill slips below roughly 1.3 times or free cash flow fails to inflect would force the market to reconsider a 49-times multiple. There is no activist, governance, or spin-off angle — Quanta is a clean S&P 500 compounder with no sum-of-the-parts discount and no asset-sale lever.
moderator_conclusion: Pass for now, conviction 2. Quanta Services is a genuinely excellent business on the right side of the most durable physical bottleneck in the AI build-out — the electric grid — with a real, widening labor and supply-chain moat, a record $48.5 billion backlog at 1.6 times book-to-bill, a strong beat-and-raise track record, and no visible secular terminal threat. The analyst credibility score is 4 of 5, with a strengthening trajectory. None of that is the issue. The issue is price. At roughly $702, or roughly 49 times forward earnings and 42 times EBITDA on a roughly 5.6% operating-margin, 8.5% return-on-invested-capital contractor whose 2026 free-cash-flow guide is only roughly $500 million — a free-cash-flow yield below 0.5% — the AI-grid opportunity is the most fully paid for in the entire grid cohort. Base-case intrinsic fair value is roughly $505 (range $470 to $540); the stock trades roughly 30 to 40% above that, which means upside to fair value is negative and gains exist only if the current high multiple persists through flawless 20%-plus compounding. Applying the portfolio's stated rules: the marquee catalyst already fired and the stock already ran to an all-time high — penalized per the 'already ran, out of window' rule. The thesis depends on the same single shared factor — AI and utility capital-spending durability — as the entire scale-out group, which fails the requirement for independent return drivers; this is the least independent name to add to a basket already saturated with that exposure. The tape is stretched and fully invested (fund manager cash at the 3.9% contrarian sell signal), the worst environment in which to chase a crowded 49-times momentum long. The catalyst status is a dated but soft catalyst with no contractual payout — the partnership is a serially extendable framework, not a single dated binding event; future awards are template extensions. Even stripping the catalyst overlay, the valuation versus intrinsic fair value is unattractive: a wide moat does not rescue a 30 to 40% premium to intrinsic on a moderate-return-on-capital base. This is the right business at the wrong price at the wrong moment. Watchlist it and re-engage on a book-to-bill or free-cash-flow flush toward $450 to $500.
interrogator_dossier:
Quanta Services (PWR) — Analyst Forensic Dossier

Prepared for the scale-out compute build-out review. Grid basket. Sources: Q1 2026 earnings call and transcript excerpts, full-year 2025 release, Q1 2026 press release, current market data — all live-verified June 2026.

Narrative Arc
Quanta's story has transformed from a cyclical electric utility contractor with a pipeline and communications grab-bag into the primary EPC labor and execution platform for the electrification and AI-load super-cycle. Three distinct shifts have occurred. First, from breadth to electrification focus: reporting collapsed into two segments — Electric Infrastructure Solutions (which absorbed Renewables) at roughly 81% of revenue and Underground and Infrastructure at roughly 19%. The company now positions itself as the labor and supply-chain certainty layer beneath the grid build-out. Second, the data-center and AI thesis went from absent to load-bearing in roughly 18 months; CEO Duke Austin describes the addressable market as a trillion-dollar-plus opportunity growing more than 100% annually, and Quanta has only been pursuing it for about a year and a half yet it is already a large business. Third, from winning work to securing the supply chain: the American Electric Power 765-kilovolt partnership announced in November 2025 and the vertical integration into transformer and equipment supply reframe the competitive moat around execution certainty, labor certainty, and supply-chain certainty bundled together. Quietly de-emphasized: legacy communications and pipeline work; Renewables is now framed as a backlog contributor without political emphasis.

Claims Versus Financials
The record, broad-based backlog claim is corroborated: $48.5 billion in Q1 2026 versus $43.98 billion at year-end 2025 versus $39.2 billion at mid-2025, with a book-to-bill ratio of roughly 1.6 times sustained. The blowout quarter and raised guidance claim is corroborated: Q1 2026 revenue $7.87 billion (up 26% year over year), adjusted earnings per share $2.68 (up 51%), and full-year guidance raised to $34.7 to $35.2 billion in revenue and $13.55 to $14.25 in adjusted earnings per share. The data-center claim of 100%-plus growth is partially verifiable: the direction is corroborated by the breadth of backlog (fewer than 25% from the initial American Electric Power partnership), but the magnitude is management assertion, not a disclosed segment line. The Underground double-digit margin aspiration is aspirational: current operating margin there is 7.3%. The strong free-cash-flow claim carries tension: 2025 free cash flow was $1.67 billion but the 2026 guide is only $500 million, reflecting working-capital drag. Margins are honestly stated: operating roughly 5.6%, gross roughly 15.1%, net roughly 3.7%, Electric segment 10.3%. Returns are moderate: return on invested capital roughly 8.3 to 8.7%, return on equity roughly 13.5% — barely clearing the cost of capital. The multiple pays for growth durability, not return on capital.

Tone and Confidence Trajectory
Rising and offensive. Austin says utilities are being asked to double in size. Acquisitions are the primary use of capital, with expectations to complete deals in the next nine months. The supply-constrained framing — a journeyman makes a journeyman; money cannot do that — positions the skilled-labor bottleneck as the competitive moat. Confidence is corroborated by guidance raises. The candid operational notes — 36-month transformer lead times, gas-generation risk in five years, queuing as a moving target — are honest disclosures, not defensive deflections.

Guidance Credibility
High. The Q1 2026 beat was roughly 30% above consensus and the full-year guide was raised. Full-year 2025 delivered $28.48 billion in revenue and $10.75 in adjusted earnings per share. The free-cash-flow framing for 2026 is conservative at $500 million with expressed confidence in landing at the higher end. The key watch item is 2026 free-cash-flow conversion. Reliability: high.

Analyst Pressure Points
Free-cash-flow conversion and the gap between earnings per share and actual cash generated; data-center revenue disclosure (management gives market-size color but no segment-level revenue line); gas-generation permit timing (recurring comment of coming soon); and margin ceiling (whether Underground reaches double digits and whether Electric can sustain above 10%).

Red Flags and Green Flags
Green: record $48.5 billion backlog with a book-to-bill of roughly 1.6 times; beat-and-raise with roughly twice the earnings leverage on 26% revenue growth; a genuine new data-center vector layered on a defensible core; and labor plus transformer integration deepening the moat. Red: 2026 free cash flow of $500 million versus 2025's $1.67 billion (capital-hungry growth); return on invested capital at roughly 8.5%; 49 times forward earnings and roughly 42 times EBITDA (optionality fully paid for); acquisition-led capital allocation introducing integration and goodwill risk; and gas backlog gated by permits.

Hidden Signals
The shift to a certainty triad in management vocabulary — execution certainty, labor certainty, supply-chain certainty — is a risk-transfer pricing-power claim. Vertical integration into equipment (management's phrase: right in the middle on both equipment) widens the moat up the value stack. The fact that fewer than 25% of new backlog comes from the initial American Electric Power partnership de-risks single-customer concentration and confirms the template has replicated broadly. Acquisitions explicitly ranked ahead of buybacks and dividends signals a large opportunity set but also active integration risk.

Capital-Allocation Assessment
Trajectory strengthening. The single most important thing to weigh: Quanta is a thin-margin, moderate-return-on-invested-capital EPC labor business — roughly 8.5% return on capital — at a high-growth multiple of roughly 49 times forward earnings and roughly 42 times EBITDA. Business quality is good but not exceptional; demand durability is excellent; valuation already prices in years of flawless AI-grid execution. The risk is that the thesis is correct and already in the price. Moat: scale-cost and intangible (irreplaceable skilled-trades labor at roughly 5,000 to 6,000 journeymen per year, prefabrication advantages, equipment-supply integration) — wide and protective of franchise and market share, stable on the evidence of return on invested capital holding around 8.5%. Secular threat: none material. Quanta is on the winning side of electrification; the only structural risk is a multi-year AI and utility capital-spending retrenchment, which would impair growth but not terminal viability.

Management credibility 4 of 5; trajectory strengthening; moat wide and real; moat trend stable; secular threat none.
skeptic_kill_fact: No single fact breaks the pass recommendation. The bear-tilted pass — wait for the wash-out, conviction 2, right business at the wrong price — survives every primary-source check. Q1 2026 revenue of $7.87 billion up 26%, adjusted earnings per share of $2.68 up 51%, the record $48.5 billion backlog, the raised 2026 full-year guidance, the American Electric Power 765-kilovolt partnership announced November 5 2025, net debt of roughly $5.5 billion, approximately 152 million shares outstanding, a price of roughly $702 to $719 with an all-time high of $785 in May 2026, and a forward price-to-earnings ratio of roughly 52 to 54 times all verify from primary sources. The central argument — that AI-grid optionality is fully paid for on a roughly 52-times multiple for an 8.5% return-on-capital contractor that has already run, and that Quanta is the least independent name to add to a basket already saturated with AI capital-spending exposure — holds. However, one load-bearing bear figure is wrong (see corrections). The error makes the name less unattractive than the debate states, not more, so it cannot upgrade the conviction; but it must be corrected.
skeptic_corrections: Stale free-cash-flow anchor — load-bearing and factually wrong. The bear thesis, the section on where we disagree with the market, the risky-window section, and the analyst dossier all assert that 2026 free-cash-flow guidance is just roughly $500 million, implying a free-cash-flow yield below 0.5% and that growth is consuming working capital without inflection. The primary source — Quanta's Q1 2026 press release and Form 8-K filed April 30 2026 — states that 2026 free-cash-flow guidance is $1.55 to $2.05 billion (operating cash flow guidance $2.35 to $2.85 billion). The $500 million figure was a stale initial guide that was superseded. The real free-cash-flow yield at roughly $108 billion in market cap is approximately 1.6 to 1.7%, roughly three-and-a-half times the bear figure, and the roughly $1.8 billion midpoint represents growth over 2025's $1.67 billion — not a working-capital-consuming decline. The earnings-to-cash-gap pillar of the bear case collapses at the corrected number. The pass recommendation still stands on valuation — roughly 52 times forward earnings, the stock trading roughly 30 to 40% above the base intrinsic estimate of roughly $505 — and on the resolution-driver-independence argument. But the cash-economics case must be restated at the correct roughly 1.6% free-cash-flow yield, which materially softens the bear. Minor items: net debt is roughly $5.5 billion (the debate file says roughly $6 billion) and the share count is roughly 152 million (the debate file says roughly 150 million) — both immaterial to the conclusion.
skeptic_evidence: Quanta Services, 'Quanta Services Reports First Quarter 2026 Results,' PRNewswire/company 8-K, 2026-04-30: Q1-26 revenue $7.87B (vs $6.23B, +26.3%), GAAP $1.45/dil sh, adjusted diluted EPS $2.68 (+50.6%), record backlog $48.5B; FY26 guidance revenue $34.7-35.2B, adj EPS $13.55-14.25, adj EBITDA $3.49-3.65B, and FREE CASH FLOW $1.55-2.05B / operating cash flow $2.35-2.85B (refutes the ~$500M FCF anchor).

AEP & Quanta Services, 'AEP and Quanta Services Announce Strategic Partnership to Advance Transmission and Power Infrastructure,' PRNewswire / aep.com, 2025-11-05: long-term Cooperation & Commitment Agreement on 765kV/HV transmission EPC plus a Development Services Agreement to expand domestic transformer/circuit-breaker manufacturing, supporting AEP's $72B capital plan incl. data-center load — verifies the marquee catalyst already fired Nov-2025.

stockanalysis.com / Q1-26 10-Q balance sheet (Mar 31 2026): total debt ~$5.89B, cash ~$365M → net debt ~$5.5B; total equity $9.14B — corroborates the ~$6B net-debt / ~1.45x leverage frame (no net cash, no special-situation overlay).

GuruFocus / FinanceCharts, June 2026: PWR forward P/E ~52-54x; price ~$702-719 with ATH $785 (2026-05-06) — confirms the name has already run and the AI-grid optionality is the most pre-paid of the GRID EPC cohort.

scale.role: Indispensable grid labor-and-execution layer — the contractors who physically build/connect the power.
scale.rationale: Genuine moat (skilled-labor scarcity, record $48.5B backlog, AEP 765kV partnership) — a strong LEVER on the un-substitutable execution layer, though partly broad-infrastructure rather than only AI. Skeptic verdict CONFIRMED_WITH_CORRECTIONS, and the correction cuts FOR the name: the bear's stale '~$500M FCF / sub-0.5% yield' anchor was false — real FY26 FCF guide is $1.55-2.05B (~1.6% yield, growing) — so only a small ~8pt haircut off a ~66 franchise (the PASS now rests on valuation alone). Base ~$505 is ~28% below spot at ~52x fwd; pay-up risk into a known backlog, wait.
scale.demand_durability: High — grid build-out labor, multi-year; partly broad-infrastructure (transmission, utilities), not only AI.
